segments.text:
Standard Potentials Potentiometer
The instrument that is used to measure the voltage of a galvanic cell is a potentiometer (also often called a voltmeter ).
Voltage tells us how much work can be done by electrons flowing through the wire connecting the half-cells.
When electrons flow from the − to the + side of the voltmeter (as shown in the figure), the voltage is positive.
When electrons flow in the opposite direction, from the + to the − side of the voltmeter, the voltage is negative.
Potentiometer
(voltmeter)
Anode
half-cell
Cathode
half-cell

Practical application of galvanic cells: pH meters
When a pH probe is dipped into a solution to measure pH, a galvanic cell is created.
The pH probe constitutes one half-cell with a salt-bridge.
The solution whose pH is measured constitutes the second half-cell.
The pH meter is the potentiometer (voltmeter).
The center wire of the BNC socket is the positive input.
The outer connection of the BNC socket is the negative input.

Measured cell potential
The potentiometer in the circuit measures the difference in electric potential (voltage) between the two metal electrodes.
E = E + − E −
E + is the potential of the electrode attached to the positive terminal.
E − is the potential of the electrode attached to the negative terminal.

Standard conditions for galvanic cells
Under standard conditions:
All reactant concentrations (for dissolved substances) and pressures (for gases) are adjusted to ensure that the activity of each reactant equals one
All product concentrations (for dissolved substances) and pressures (for gases) are adjusted to ensure that the activity of each reaction product equals one
The temperature is fixed at a well-defined value (for example, T = 25°C ).

Standard cell potential
When all components of both half-cells are present at standard concentrations, pressures, and temperatures, then the measured cell potential is called the standard cell potential ( E o ) .
is the standard reduction potential of the electrode attached to the positive terminal.
is the standard reduction potential of the electrode attached to the negative terminal.

Predicting standard cell potential
Identify the two half-reactions. (Reverse the oxidation half-reaction so that it is written as a reduction.)
Look up the standard reduction potential for each half-reaction.
Calculate the standard cell voltage.
E ° = 0.799 V − (−0.402 V)
E ° = 1.201 V

How are standard half-reaction potentials measured? (1 of 2)
Consider the half-reaction for the reduction of Ag + to Ag( s ).
The standard reduction potential for this half-reaction is 0.799 V.
How did the scientists who made the table measure this standard reduction potential for the Ag + /Ag( s ) half-reaction?

How are standard half-reaction potentials measured? (2 of 2)
The standard hydrogen electrode (S.H.E.) is a half-reaction whose standard reduction potential is defined to be 0 at 25°C . The S.H.E. is used as a reference half-reaction to measure other standard half-reaction potentials.
Figure 14-8

Nernst Equation How is a nonstandard potential calculated?
The cell voltage for standard cells can be readily predicted using the tabulated half-reaction standard reduction potentials.
The superscripted “ ° ” indicates standard conditions.
Under nonstandard conditions, the equation becomes
E = E + − E −
How are the nonstandard reduction potentials E + and E − calculated?

The Nernst Equation
The Nernst equation is used to calculate the reduction potential for each half-cell ( E + or E − ) under nonstandard conditions.
For the reaction a A + n e −
b B the Nernst equation is
R is the gas constant (8.314 J/(K ∙ mol) = 8.314 (V ∙ C) / (K ∙ mol))
T is the temperature (K)
F is the Faraday constant
n is the number of electrons in the half-reaction
is the activity of species i
Q is the reaction quotient

Solution: The argument of the logarithm in equation B is the square of the argument in equation A. Equation B can be rewritten in the following manner:

Example: Multiplying a Half-Reaction Does Not Change the Half-Cell Potential E (3 of 4)
Solution: A mathematical property of logarithms is log⁡ a b = b log⁡ a .  Rewriting the right-hand term as log a 2 = 2 log⁡ a , we can remove the square exponent from the logarithm:
We just showed that Nernst equation B is the same as Nernst equation A. Multiplying a half-reaction by any factor does not change the potential E .

Nernst Equation for a Complete Reaction (1 of 2)
Stepwise strategy for setting up a Nernst equation for a complete reaction
Step 1 Write reduction half-reactions for both half-cells and look up
and
in a table of standard reduction potentials. In preparation for balancing the overall redox reaction, scale the half-reactions so that they contain the same number of electrons, n . When you scale a half reaction, do not scale E ° .
Step 2 Write a Nernst equation for the right half-cell, which is attached to the positive terminal of the potentiometer. This is E + . The symbol E + does not indicate whether the electrode is the anode or the cathode, only that it is attached to the positive terminal.
Step 3 Write a Nernst equation for the left half-cell, which is attached to the negative terminal of the potentiometer. This is E − . The symbol E − does not indicate whether the electrode is the anode or the cathode, only that it is attached to the negative terminal.
Step 4 Find the net cell voltage by subtraction.
Step 5 Write the net cell reaction by subtracting the left half-reaction from the right half-reaction.

Nernst Equation for a Complete Reaction (2 of 2)
Nernst equation for a complete reaction
E = E + − E −
Converting into the base 10 logarithm and inserting T = 298.15 K (25.00°C) gives a simplified Nernst equation for predicting voltages at room temperature.
Electrons flow spontaneously through the circuit from the more negative electrode to the more positive electrode.
If E is positive, electrons flow through the wire from the left-hand electrode to the right-hand electrode.
If E is negative, electrons flow through the wire from the right-hand electrode to the left-hand electrode.

E° and the Equilibrium Constant
Galvanic cells produce electricity when they are not at equilibrium.
The voltage of a good battery can be predicted by the Nernst equation.
When a battery has died, the chemicals inside have reached equilibrium ( Q = K) and the battery voltage has dropped to zero ( E = 0 V)
At equilibrium, the net reaction Nernst equation simplifies to the following form:
at 25°C
𝐾 = 10 𝑛𝐸°/0.059 16 at 25°C

Example: Using E° to Find the Equilibrium Constant (3 of 4)
Solution: Then find E° for the net reaction
and compute the equilibrium constant with Equation 14-25:
K = 10 (2)(0.432)/(0.059 16) = 4 × 10 14
A modest value of E° produces a large equilibrium constant. The value of K is correctly expressed with one significant figure because E° has three digits. Two are used for the exponent (14), and one is left for the multiplier (4).

Biochemists use formal potential, E °′
Whenever H + appears in a redox reaction, or whenever reactants or products are acids or bases, reduction potentials are pH dependent.
The pH inside a plant or animal cell is about 7; redox potentials that apply at pH 0 are not appropriate.
The standard potential for a redox reaction is defined for a galvanic cell in which all activities are unity.
The formal potential is the reduction potential that applies under a specified set of conditions.
Biochemists call the formal potential at pH 7 E ° ′.

Relation between E ° and E °′
where
F represents the formal concentration of A and B. [A] and [B] are converted to F A and F B using the fractional composition equations in Section 10-5
To measure E °′ adjust the formal concentrations of the oxidized and reduced species so they are equal and adjust the pH to 7. The log term is zero under these conditions and the measured potential (versus S.H.E.) is E °′.
